title: Psalms 42
subtitle: As the Deer Pants for Streams of Water

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Longing for the Temple

heb.paragraph: "As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God. My soul thirsts (ṣāməʾâ) for God, for the living God. When can I go and meet with God?" (vv.1–2). Book II opens (Psalms 42–72, the "Elohistic Psalter" where God is called Elohim rather than YHWH). A Son of Korah psalm. The psalmist is exiled from the temple, thirsting for God’s presence. "My tears have been my food day and night, while people say to me all day long, ‘Where is your God?’" (v.3). The refrain: "Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God" (v.5).

notes.note: "As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God." The most famous thirst-image in Scripture. Physical dehydration as metaphor for spiritual longing.
"Where is your God?" The taunt of the enemy. The absent God is the psalmist’s deepest wound.

"Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God, for I will yet praise him, my Saviour and my God." The refrain (repeated in 42:11 and 43:5): self-counsel. The psalmist preaches to his own soul.

refs.note: Jesus's invitation to the thirsty to come and drink transforms the psalm's desperate longing into fulfilled promise.
The promise of water from the spring of life freely given completes the psalm's thirsting for God.
Those who hunger and thirst for righteousness being filled answers the psalmist's unfulfilled longing.

hist.context: Psalms 42-43 were likely a single composition later divided. This psalm opens Book II of the Psalter and introduces the 'Sons of Korah' collection. The Korahites were Levitical temple singers (1 Chr 6:31-38). The reference to the Jordan, Mount Hermon, and Mount Mizar suggests the psalmist is in the far north, exiled from Jerusalem's temple.

heb.paragraph: "Deep calls to deep (təhōm) in the roar of your waterfalls; all your waves and breakers have swept over me" (v.7). The image of being overwhelmed: wave upon wave. The deeps of suffering call to each other across the psalmist’s soul. Yet: "By day the LORD directs his love, at night his song is with me" (v.8). Day-love and night-song — even in the flood, God’s love and music persist.

notes.note: "Deep calls to deep." The waves are God’s — "YOUR waves and breakers." Even the overwhelming flood belongs to God. The suffering is not random; it is under divine jurisdiction.
"By day the LORD directs his love, at night his song is with me." The most remarkable claim: in the deepest water, God sends both love and music. Day and night, the provisions arrive.
"Why, my soul, are you downcast?" The refrain returns. The psalmist has not been rescued; he is still in exile. But the self-counsel continues: hope in God.

refs.note: The identical refrain ('Why so downcast, O my soul?') confirms the literary unity of Psalms 42-43.
Jesus's declaration that his soul is overwhelmed with sorrow in Gethsemane echoes the psalm's downcast soul.
Paul's 'hard pressed but not crushed' captures the psalm's oscillation between despair and hope.

hist.context: The refrain structure (vv.5, 11; 43:5) creates a three-stanza poem of lament and self-encouragement. 'Deep calls to deep' evokes primordial chaos waters — the psalmist feels overwhelmed by forces beyond control. The taunting question 'Where is your God?' assumes that divine absence proves divine impotence, a standard ancient Near Eastern theological challenge.

ppl.name: The Sons of Korah
ppl.role: Levitical worship guild and attributed authors (superscription)
ppl.text: The Korahites were Levitical singers descended from Korah, whose rebellion against Moses ended in divine judgment (Num 16). That a worship guild bears the rebel's name testifies to grace across generations. They authored Psalms 42-49, 84-85, 87-88 — a collection marked by Zion theology and longing for God's presence. Psalms 42-43 form a single poem (same refrain, no separate superscription for 43).

ppl.name: God (Elohim)
ppl.role: The living God whose absence the psalmist mourns (vv.1-2,8)
ppl.text: The psalmist thirsts for 'the living God' (El chai, v.2) — not an abstract deity but one who actively sustains life. The epithet deliberately contrasts with dead idols. God's 'love' (chesed) is present by day and his 'song' by night (v.8), suggesting that even in felt absence, covenant love persists objectively. The repeated question 'Why have you forgotten me?' (v.9) holds God accountable to his own promises.

ppl.name: The Psalmist (Exiled Worshiper)
ppl.role: A Levite cut off from temple worship, battling despair (vv.1-6,9-11)
ppl.text: Displaced to the region of the Jordan, Mount Hermon, and Mount Mizar (v.6) — the far north, away from Jerusalem — this worship leader remembers leading processions to God's house (v.4). His tears are his food 'day and night' (v.3), and enemies taunt 'Where is your God?' (v.3,10). The threefold refrain ('Why so downcast, O my soul?' vv.5,11; 43:5) is self-counsel: the psalmist preaches hope to his own despair, modeling faith as argument against feeling.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI say to God my Rock, “Why have you forgotten me? Why must I go about mourning, oppressed by the enemy?”ESVI say to God, my rock: \u201cWhy have you forgotten me? Why do I go mourning because of the oppression of the enemy?\u201dNIVThese things I remember as I pour out my soul: how I used to go to the house of God under the protection of the Mighty One with shouts of joy and praise among the festive throng.ESVThese things I remember, as I pour out my soul: how I would go with the throng and lead them in procession to the house of God with glad shouts and songs of praise, a multitude keeping festival.

rec.text: ""As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God." The most famous thirst-image in Scripture. Physical dehydration as metaphor for spiritual longing."

rows.label: Deep Calls to Deep — 42:6–11
rows.text: Waves and breakers. Day-love, night-song. The refrain: "Why are you downcast, my soul?"

hebtext.note: "As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you, my God. My soul thirsts (ṣāməʾâ) for God, for the living God. When can I go and meet with God?" (vv.1–2). Book II opens (Psalms 42–72, the "Elohistic Psalter" where God is called Elohim rather than YHWH). A Son of Korah psalm. The psalmist is exiled from the temple, thirsting for God’s presence. "My tears have been my food day and night, while people say to me all day long, ‘Where is your God?’" (v.3). The refrain: "Why, my soul, are you downcast? Put your hope in God" (v.5).

"Deep calls to deep (təhōm) in the roar of your waterfalls; all your waves and breakers have swept over me" (v.7). The image of being overwhelmed: wave upon wave. The deeps of suffering call to each other across the psalmist’s soul. Yet: "By day the LORD directs his love, at night his song is with me" (v.8). Day-love and night-song — even in the flood, God’s love and music persist.

themes.note: Faith peaks: self-counsel in depression — "put your hope in God." Mercy: day-love and night-song in the flood. Worship: Book II opens with longing for God’s presence.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on 'As the deer pants for streams of water, so my soul pants for you,' consider asking God whether your longing for Him matches that intensity — or whether you have so many other streams that your spiritual thirst has dulled.
